speaker: Ernest Burke
rights: University of Baltimore, educational and research use
text: In 1998, I, Steve Bachman will be interviewing Ernest Bach. This interview is being conducted in Mr. Bach's home, which is located in Perry Hall, Maryland. The purpose of this interview is to learn about the nigger league through the oral testimony of Ernest Bach, who played baseball for the Eli Chines. The University of Baltimore and the Bay Bridge Museum are working together on this project. Mr. Ernest Bach, on behalf of the University of Baltimore and the Bay Bridge Museum, may we have your consent to record, transcribe, and use this interview. I, Ernest Bach, give you permission to grant your uh... Okay. Thank you. Okay, Mr. Bach, where were you born? I've been born in Perryville, Maryland, 1924. As a child, what recreational activities were available to you? Well, when I was a child, we had a stick we rolled a rim or a tire, and uh... that was only any uh... athletic that I've done and all the rest of them in my neighborhood. I was only black that lived in Perryville, Maryland, my family, and uh... that's only recreation, white or black head. Tell me about your first exposure to baseball and when you first played. Well, I entered into the Marine Corps in 1942, and uh... I started playing baseball in Hawaii. There, and we went to the Pacific Championship. And from that day forth, I just kept playing and I played such a minute, Johnny Rittenby, was in the CBEs and service. And he told me as good as I played, I should be playing in the Negro League, which I didn't know anything about the Negro League. And so when I got out, I contacted the Baltimore League Giants in 42 and 46, and uh... I continued on and played with them for two years. And what made it so good, I didn't go into farm team. When I joined them, I came right up to the first string team. And I played with them for two years, and I played in the Canadian League. And getting back, as far as the Negro League is concerned, there's a lot of discrimination. A lot of times you couldn't go in restaurants to eat. You couldn't stay in hotels, proper hotels, not less you in a black neighborhood. We traveled by bus. A lot of times we had to change clothes and buses. And get out and play, and sometimes I remember even gotten behind a scoreboarder, or a billboard and change clothes to a regular play baseball. And there's a lot of parts you went to that we wouldn't allow to use the locker rooms and showers and all that sort of thing. It was, it was dead. It was really dead. But overall, we still had fun donnies thing. We got in the bus, we had jokers and guys that tell stories. It was one big happy family. I can remember I back in those days, it didn't slice bread, they had a whole loaf of bread. And you go to store and get a, we call it a chunk of baloney, but it was a real thick piece of baloney and an onion and aided. And a bread, I can remember when I dug out the center of the bread, poured a can of baked beans in it and take the inner, the inner and the juice up and ate that and then got out and pitched nine innings. I mean nine innings, no reliever, no middle man, I pitched nine innings. And that's where it was throughout the whole late. And I don't know, it's just, there's just something else. But we survived. As far as Jackie Robinson turned, I put him on a pedestal, although he wasn't the best ball player in the Negro League. But he was the best man to handle a situation with Brantwicky picked him. He had in college, four, four, four, four, four letter. He's also a lieutenant in the Army. And he's been the trippin', he knew what was happening, what was going on. I think he's the only black, could handle a situation, the way he did. And as I said once, I'll say it again, I put him on a pedestal because, for him, it wouldn't be any blacks in the Negro and the white ladies as of today. And all the blacks that's in the majors today, they should honor Jackie Robinson and put him on a pedestal because it wouldn't be there. How did your family, friends and acquaintances react to you as they became aware that you remember the bottom-up of your EDI giants? Well, I had family and friends. They really was happy for me that I was playing organized baseball. Even down, I had the team to come up and have a degree from Maryland and play the little small teams there a couple of times. And you should see the people turn out white and black. I mean, the little stadium was full of people lined up all down the lines. And everywhere that I came there and the DDI giants that I played for to pull me like giants, the team I played for was coming to have a degree. It's to play the little minor teams and everybody said, I never dreamed of a team like that coming year-plant. And I was responsible for it. That made me feel good at the people responsible. You mentioned travel conditions. Tell me what travel conditions were like when you played outside of Baltimore. Well, when we played outside of Baltimore, travel conditions like I said before, it was bad because we stayed, went to a small town, especially a southern town and pulled up to a gas station or something like that. They would come out and say, what do you niggers want here? What do you want? And we say we want gas. And they tell us to pull over to the side. We'll get you in a minute. And I can remember one time we stayed at a service station almost two hours before he came out and said, you niggers back to bus up here and I'll give you some gas. And we had a bus and it's hold 30, 40 gallon of gas. And we asked him to fill the tank up. He said, well, I'll give you five gallon of gas and get your ass out of here. We don't want you niggers down here anyway. And that's sort of things. You know, you had to hold your tongue. And for people to come up and say things like that to you. I mean, it's really something else. I could never believe that people would be so harsh and so evil against another man because the color of these skin. What travel, where would you stay? Well, when we went to large towns or large cities, they had the black neighborhood. And they had hotels. And a lot of people didn't have hotels. They had rooms that our owners used to rent for years and years and years. Every time we went there, so it was, you know, they knew we were coming. So they had to rooms ready for each year for us. We just go at different people's houses. And like when you're down south, we stayed at houses. And there's one lady that owned a farm her and her husband. And she used to feed. It makes no different. What ball team came there? She used to feed them all. Though we paid for it. But it was just like, I don't know, a being in a camp or something like that because they had the tables set up with milk and just good food. And we go there two times a day or three times a day. And you know, when we traveled, we got $2 a day for eating money. Well, people say back in those days, $2 went a long way. But you take $2 a day and try to eat three meals off of it. It's impossible for you. Three meals, not less you eat. The way I said you were eating with lunch meat, bologna or can of baked beans and a loaf of bread. And I've, like I said, about the baked beans. I've got a large piece of bologna and an onion and a loaf of bread. Take a bite of bologna and a bite of onion and a bite of bread. And you know, that's the way we had to eat and that's the way we did. How did the pay as a baseball player compared to the pay non baseball players earned for various jobs? Well, I can speak for myself. I got $350 a month, $2 a day for eating money. And that's no comparison of what the white bowl players were playing. Deep down in my heart and it proved to be true that we were much better ball players and the white because we used to barm star against the white all star teams. They used to pick up a all star team. We used to play. We used to beat them 60% of the games we played at Born Cume was the president of the National League and American League and he stopped it. He put a stop to it. It was an embarrassment. He didn't say that but when he put a stop to it it was an embarrassment for a bunch of black guys to come up and be a bunch of all star teams you might say. Do you remember some of the players and the major leagues that were playing these all star games? It's been so long ago. I know Al Kailin. I know Ted Williams. For Craig's it's been so long ago. I can't think of all of them but those ones are actually stuck in my mind. And what ways did the Nigger League affect the Black community? Well before or after before they went to the majors or both. Well, when we played in the Nigger League before Jackie broke the club area they supported us 100%. 100% the stands are always full. Regardless of where you went. We had an East and West game that we played in Chicago, from East Stadium. And that stadium was packed. I mean it was standing room only and it was packed. Had people come from all over the world to see the old star game there. And as a phrase was made when Jackie Robinson broke the color barrier it says it was a damn shame for 500 or 800 people to lose your job over one man to break the color barrier because all the good players went into the major leagues and that's when the Nigger League folded. And you must remember as quiet as it was kept we had three or four white owners that owned teams in the Nigger of the day. And I wanted to press on that. They were making money and let us get treated the way we were and they were followed through to help us one way or the other. And just fortunate enough I played for a team where a black owner owned a team and it was real good too because he knew what we went through and he was from Mexico Tennessee, Vernon Green. And he knew what we went through and he knew what the situation was. Did the season coincide with that of the major leagues? Did the season parallel with the major leagues? Yes, yes, yes. The only difference were the major leagues they played one or two days and traveled and had a day off here and a day off there. When we weren't planning the league we played every day to an order to make money so we get our pay. And that's the way we played every day and we traveled three, four hundred miles between games, different cities and play. Sometimes I know that we got on the bus here in Baltimore. We went to Cleveland and maybe played a double header and we left Cleveland and maybe went to Chicago and played a triple header where there are two teams playing, you know, fourteen played. You know, and we'd go to Texas and make this circle and end up back in Baltimore. And I'd tell you, riding in a school bus, there was no picnic, but in other seats that laid back or anything. Later on in the years they got other buses where you had to get climbing seats, you know, that laid back and that was something else. I was just like getting off the floor, sleeping in the feather bed. There was just a much different good air condition. What job did you have before being a bigger player? Well, I went in Rincor when I was seventeen and I lived in Canada for quite a number of years. So I really didn't have a job, you know. And when I came out and I finished playing baseball, I worked for Henry Knott here in the Baltimore City. He was a construction outfit. I used to operate heavy equipment there. And I worked for him. I was in the Teamsters Union. I worked for them for thirty years and retired. When I cried down, I was teaching professional tennis. I was tennis brother now. I teach it seven days a week. When did you get involved with tennis? It was shortly after I retired from Henry Knott. I went to all the best camps for him to teach me how to teach. And I just went from there. Had you played tennis? Well, I played tennis, but I never taken it seriously. I just hit the ball as hard as I could. And maybe just walk off the court and keep going. But I really found it to be a good sport and you can play it to your ninety years old or hundred. What impacted World War II have on yourself and on the Nigger League? Well, a lot of ball players was in the Second World War alone with me. And I tell you, by living in Canada for quite a long time, it was kind of rough on me because the forest segregation was concerned. And people calling you Niggers and Black and you couldn't go here and you couldn't go there. It was quite an impression. And then you take a lot of blacks. They thought I was trying to be above myself because I had a different language. I spoke a little different. You know, the whites didn't want me and the blacks, they were a little weary. I mean, just like I tell everybody, I had like a piece of meat between two pieces of bread. I didn't know what size I was on, you know. So it was kind of rough for me. But in the Marine Corps, they called you Nigger and Darky Cotton Picker. They didn't want you in the Marine Corps and they said you didn't have enough brains to the far rifle, but yet still we went on the right range and shot expert and sharp shooter and things like that. It was... I just made up my mind when I went to Marine Corps. I volunteered for the Marine Corps. And they tried to break me, not me, all of us was in there doing it that particular time. They tried to break us, but we all seemed to have the same thought in our mind. We were there for a reason. And nobody was going to break us regardless of what they did to us. So that's what we did. And I have a daughter just got out of Marine Corps. She just put 20 years into Marine Corps. And she often says, if it hadn't been for you and people like you, I wouldn't have been here today. And I've talked to other Marines, other young Marines, and I've got speeches on the base of Black Marines and told them the hardship that we went through for them now, the opportunity that they have now. And they really appreciate it. You know, you're just standing up. How did the Negro League Players react in 1947 to the news of Jackie Robinson about the color barrier? Well, they act great. They were happy. But as I always say, you know, if you open up a crate out, there are always going to be one or two bedrooms in there. You know, not the same way with the Negro League Players. There's always one or two. And I should have made it. I should have been picked. But deep down in my heart, I think, Branch Rickie did the right thing, but picking Jackie Robinson overall, other Negro League Players. Because you must realize when Jackie went, broke the color barrier and it went to Montreal to play. All they wanted him to do is fight back or call names or something. And they said, well, that's what you expect out of Blacks. That's what you expect to be in an honest situation. And he didn't do that. And that's what broke the color barrier. That's what went into the white society that he was there. See, a white society said, the past time of baseball was for whites and whites only. And then when Jackie Robinson went up there and broke the color barrier, they just opened their mouth and couldn't believe it. That's why they wrote, threatened the letters, death threats, and calling all kinds of names. And I can't believe that Baltimore City that treated him so bad. That she was real life. Baltimore, who went to the first southern states in the state of America and in the state history. So it's a barrier, you know, and so I don't know. It's just unbelievable that I said before. It's unbelievable that you see one person, hate another person just because he's a color of his skin. You know, you take doing a war, take prince and doing a war. They work side by side. You know, Martin putting together planes and so on and so forth. Any plant they work side by side. And as soon everything was over, they went right back to the hating. That's what I don't understand. I watch TV and see where the hurricane went through. Where a tornado. And you see blacks down there and the whites hand to hand trying to help each other. I know it's really going to get situated. You're ready to call your nigger or a dorky and they won't want you in the store. They want you in the house and things like that. That's what I don't understand. I never will understand it. You mentioned in a pre-interview that you played in Canadian League. Can you tell me how that came about? Well I went in Canadian League in 1449 and I played for St. James. St. James Braves. And it was a good league up there. Very good league. And we had a white end black that played in it. So B was a good league. Everybody got along together. I knew I had one manager that kind of got off the peak a little bit. We overcame that. You know. He asked me to, I used the towels that the other white boys was using and he got me a cup to drink out of it so I wouldn't drink out of the same cup. But before the season was over I was drinking out of the same cup and using the same towels because the type of men was up there. They didn't let it continue. Were the players in the Canadian League mostly Canadian? No. No. We had a few Canadians. What ways did your experiences as a Nigger League player change your life and your future? Well, just like I said when I first started playing baseball in the Nigger League I signed a contract and I was doing something that I lacked and I was getting paid for it. It made me feel good to walk into Yankee Stadium with all the people there and be pitching to the Yankee Stadium or any of the major league ballpark as far as that concerned. It made me feel wonderful. What type of attendance is for how many people would attend the games? I'd say 30, 40,000. And you mentioned to me that you're doing a lot of things with the Nigger League now and the schools can tell. Yeah, well I go to schools and give talks and I go to car shows and sell the Nigger League stuff. I've got bats on my own and I still have pictures and baseball cards. And just a little bit of everything of the Nigger League that I have myself. Okay, Mr. Barb, thank you very much for your time. Well, thank you. Turn off the tape. I'm resuming my interview with Mr. Ernest Bark. Mr. Bark, could you tell me about spring training, how a pale black man would go about trying out for a team and so forth? Well, each team had its own town to spring training, a southern town because that's where it was warm. We used to spring train every March in Nashville, Tennessee. Now, to order to make the team, it was just like the majors. If a scout or somebody didn't see you, and offered you to come to Nashville, Tennessee with the ball-releague giants, you just packed your bag and came down there and take your chances. And that's what it was all about. You didn't just walk on a team just because one of the teams was playing just walked back there and said that you said that you want to play, you would have to perform in spring training and show that your ability was able enough to play in the Negro League. And there's a lot of players that came that stayed in the minors. Legs are what you call them in spring. The scouting team was the second team to the ball-releague giants and they were playing a southern leg until they were called up to the big team. How about groupies? Did you have groupies or did players have fan base and girls that would follow them trying to come to the ball to the player? Well, some guys did. They didn't follow them because they wasn't that much money to follow them. But sure enough, when you went to the same town again, you probably saw the same girl. And you had fans, you had a lot of fans and especially a lot of kids. I used to have a lot of kids. I go to a town and the kids would all gang around me because I used to talk to them. Although I didn't make much money, I used to take them out. I had my screen and stuff like that and talk to them. And that made me feel good. It made me feel wonderful. And to me, it made no difference whether it was white or black. If they hadn't been in the group that I was there, they went just like everybody else. Okay, Mr. Burke, you mentioned that when you were about 10 you moved to Canada. Can you tell me what it was like living in Canada and how that compared with the United States? Well, it was quite a thrill because you never knew you were black, you looked in a mirror. And everybody treated you regardless of what color you were. Everybody treated you the same. And I lived some people a fair called a Monarchs. And they had three sons and two girls. And they were just like brothers to me. No matter how we used to call each other brothers in the system. And they didn't get any more than I got. I didn't get any more than they got. We were all treated the same. And it was really nice. And the second world war broke out. Then I came back to the States and volunteered and joined the Marine Corps. And that was it from there. Okay, on behalf of the University of Baltimore and the Bay Bridge Museum and myself, I'd like to thank you for your time, Mr. Burke. Thank you.

segments.text: In 1998, I, Steve Bachman will be interviewing Ernest Bach.
This interview is being conducted in Mr. Bach's home, which is located in Perry Hall, Maryland.
The purpose of this interview is to learn about the nigger league through the oral testimony
of Ernest Bach, who played baseball for the Eli Chines.
The University of Baltimore and the Bay Bridge Museum are working together on this project.
Mr. Ernest Bach, on behalf of the University of Baltimore and the Bay Bridge Museum,
may we have your consent to record, transcribe, and use this interview.
I, Ernest Bach, give you permission to grant your uh...
Okay.
Thank you.
Okay, Mr. Bach, where were you born?
I've been born in Perryville, Maryland, 1924.
As a child, what recreational activities were available to you?
Well, when I was a child, we had a stick we rolled a rim or a tire, and uh...
that was only any uh... athletic that I've done and all the rest of them in my neighborhood.
I was only black that lived in Perryville, Maryland, my family, and uh...
that's only recreation, white or black head.
Tell me about your first exposure to baseball and when you first played.
Well, I entered into the Marine Corps in 1942,
and uh... I started playing baseball in Hawaii.
There, and we went to the Pacific Championship.
And from that day forth, I just kept playing and I played such a minute, Johnny Rittenby,
was in the CBEs and service.
And he told me as good as I played, I should be playing in the Negro League,
which I didn't know anything about the Negro League.
And so when I got out, I contacted the Baltimore League Giants in 42 and 46,
and uh... I continued on and played with them for two years.
And what made it so good, I didn't go into farm team.
When I joined them, I came right up to the first string team.
And I played with them for two years, and I played in the Canadian League.
And getting back, as far as the Negro League is concerned,
there's a lot of discrimination.
A lot of times you couldn't go in restaurants to eat.
You couldn't stay in hotels, proper hotels, not less you in a black neighborhood.
We traveled by bus.
A lot of times we had to change clothes and buses.
And get out and play, and sometimes I remember even gotten behind a scoreboarder,
or a billboard and change clothes to a regular play baseball.
And there's a lot of parts you went to that we wouldn't allow to use the locker rooms
and showers and all that sort of thing.
It was, it was dead.
It was really dead.
But overall, we still had fun donnies thing.
We got in the bus, we had jokers and guys that tell stories.
It was one big happy family.
I can remember I back in those days, it didn't slice bread, they had a whole loaf of bread.
And you go to store and get a, we call it a chunk of baloney,
but it was a real thick piece of baloney and an onion and aided.
And a bread, I can remember when I dug out the center of the bread,
poured a can of baked beans in it and take the inner, the inner and the juice up and ate that
and then got out and pitched nine innings.
I mean nine innings, no reliever, no middle man, I pitched nine innings.
And that's where it was throughout the whole late.
And I don't know, it's just, there's just something else.
But we survived.
As far as Jackie Robinson turned, I put him on a pedestal,
although he wasn't the best ball player in the Negro League.
But he was the best man to handle a situation with Brantwicky picked him.
He had in college, four, four, four, four, four letter.
He's also a lieutenant in the Army.
And he's been the trippin', he knew what was happening, what was going on.
I think he's the only black, could handle a situation, the way he did.
And as I said once, I'll say it again, I put him on a pedestal
because, for him, it wouldn't be any blacks in the Negro and the white ladies as of today.
And all the blacks that's in the majors today, they should honor Jackie Robinson
and put him on a pedestal because it wouldn't be there.
How did your family, friends and acquaintances react to you
as they became aware that you remember the bottom-up of your EDI giants?
Well, I had family and friends.
They really was happy for me that I was playing organized baseball.
Even down, I had the team to come up and have a degree from Maryland
and play the little small teams there a couple of times.
And you should see the people turn out white and black.
I mean, the little stadium was full of people lined up all down the lines.
And everywhere that I came there and the DDI giants that I played for
to pull me like giants, the team I played for was coming to have a degree.
It's to play the little minor teams and everybody said,
I never dreamed of a team like that coming year-plant.
And I was responsible for it.
That made me feel good at the people responsible.
You mentioned travel conditions.
Tell me what travel conditions were like when you played outside of Baltimore.
Well, when we played outside of Baltimore, travel conditions like I said before,
it was bad because we stayed, went to a small town,
especially a southern town and pulled up to a gas station or something like that.
They would come out and say, what do you niggers want here?
What do you want?
And we say we want gas.
And they tell us to pull over to the side.
We'll get you in a minute.
And I can remember one time we stayed at a service station almost two hours
before he came out and said, you niggers back to bus up here and I'll give you some gas.
And we had a bus and it's hold 30, 40 gallon of gas.
And we asked him to fill the tank up.
He said, well, I'll give you five gallon of gas and get your ass out of here.
We don't want you niggers down here anyway.
And that's sort of things.
You know, you had to hold your tongue.
And for people to come up and say things like that to you.
I mean, it's really something else.
I could never believe that people would be so harsh and so evil against another man
because the color of these skin.
What travel, where would you stay?
Well, when we went to large towns or large cities, they had the black neighborhood.
And they had hotels.
And a lot of people didn't have hotels.
They had rooms that our owners used to rent for years and years and years.
Every time we went there, so it was, you know, they knew we were coming.
So they had to rooms ready for each year for us.
We just go at different people's houses.
And like when you're down south, we stayed at houses.
And there's one lady that owned a farm her and her husband.
And she used to feed.
It makes no different.
What ball team came there?
She used to feed them all.
Though we paid for it.
But it was just like, I don't know, a being in a camp or something like that because they
had the tables set up with milk and just good food.
And we go there two times a day or three times a day.
And you know, when we traveled, we got $2 a day for eating money.
Well, people say back in those days, $2 went a long way.
But you take $2 a day and try to eat three meals off of it.
It's impossible for you.
Three meals, not less you eat.
The way I said you were eating with lunch meat, bologna or can of baked beans and a loaf
of bread.
And I've, like I said, about the baked beans.
I've got a large piece of bologna and an onion and a loaf of bread.
Take a bite of bologna and a bite of onion and a bite of bread.
And you know, that's the way we had to eat and that's the way we did.
How did the pay as a baseball player compared to the pay non baseball players earned for various
jobs?
Well, I can speak for myself.
I got $350 a month, $2 a day for eating money.
And that's no comparison of what the white bowl players were playing.
Deep down in my heart and it proved to be true that we were much better ball players
and the white because we used to barm star against the white all star teams.
They used to pick up a all star team.
We used to play.
We used to beat them 60% of the games we played at Born Cume was the president of the
National League and American League and he stopped it.
He put a stop to it.
It was an embarrassment.
He didn't say that but when he put a stop to it it was an embarrassment for a bunch of
black guys to come up and be a bunch of all star teams you might say.
Do you remember some of the players and the major leagues that were playing these all
star games?
It's been so long ago.
I know Al Kailin.
I know Ted Williams.
For Craig's it's been so long ago.
I can't think of all of them but those ones are actually stuck in my mind.
And what ways did the Nigger League affect the Black community?
Well before or after before they went to the majors or both.
Well, when we played in the Nigger League before Jackie broke the club area they supported
100% the stands are always full.
Regardless of where you went.
We had an East and West game that we played in Chicago, from East Stadium.
And that stadium was packed.
I mean it was standing room only and it was packed.
Had people come from all over the world to see the old star game there.
And as a phrase was made when Jackie Robinson broke the color barrier it says it was a
damn shame for 500 or 800 people to lose your job over one man to break the color barrier
because all the good players went into the major leagues and that's when the Nigger
League folded.
And you must remember as quiet as it was kept we had three or four white owners that owned
teams in the Nigger of the day.
And I wanted to press on that.
They were making money and let us get treated the way we were and they were followed through
to help us one way or the other.
And just fortunate enough I played for a team where a black owner owned a team and it was
real good too because he knew what we went through and he was from Mexico Tennessee,
Vernon Green.
And he knew what we went through and he knew what the situation was.
Did the season coincide with that of the major leagues?
Did the season parallel with the major leagues?
Yes, yes, yes.
The only difference were the major leagues they played one or two days and traveled and had
a day off here and a day off there.
When we weren't planning the league we played every day to an order to make money so we
get our pay.
And that's the way we played every day and we traveled three, four hundred miles between
games, different cities and play.
Sometimes I know that we got on the bus here in Baltimore.
We went to Cleveland and maybe played a double header and we left Cleveland and maybe
went to Chicago and played a triple header where there are two teams playing, you know,
fourteen played.
You know, and we'd go to Texas and make this circle and end up back in Baltimore.
And I'd tell you, riding in a school bus, there was no picnic, but in other seats that
laid back or anything.
Later on in the years they got other buses where you had to get climbing seats, you know,
that laid back and that was something else.
I was just like getting off the floor, sleeping in the feather bed.
There was just a much different good air condition.
What job did you have before being a bigger player?
Well, I went in Rincor when I was seventeen and I lived in Canada for quite a number of
years.
So I really didn't have a job, you know.
And when I came out and I finished playing baseball, I worked for Henry Knott here in the
Baltimore City.
He was a construction outfit.
I used to operate heavy equipment there.
And I worked for him.
I was in the Teamsters Union.
I worked for them for thirty years and retired.
When I cried down, I was teaching professional tennis.
I was tennis brother now.
I teach it seven days a week.
When did you get involved with tennis?
It was shortly after I retired from Henry Knott.
I went to all the best camps for him to teach me how to teach.
And I just went from there.
Had you played tennis?
Well, I played tennis, but I never taken it seriously.
I just hit the ball as hard as I could.
And maybe just walk off the court and keep going.
But I really found it to be a good sport and you can play it to your ninety years old or
hundred.
What impacted World War II have on yourself and on the Nigger League?
Well, a lot of ball players was in the Second World War alone with me.
And I tell you, by living in Canada for quite a long time, it was kind of rough on me
because the forest segregation was concerned.
And people calling you Niggers and Black and you couldn't go here and you couldn't go
there.
It was quite an impression.
And then you take a lot of blacks.
They thought I was trying to be above myself because I had a different language.
I spoke a little different.
You know, the whites didn't want me and the blacks, they were a little weary.
I mean, just like I tell everybody, I had like a piece of meat between two pieces of bread.
I didn't know what size I was on, you know.
So it was kind of rough for me.
But in the Marine Corps, they called you Nigger and Darky Cotton Picker.
They didn't want you in the Marine Corps and they said you didn't have enough brains
to the far rifle, but yet still we went on the right range and shot expert and sharp shooter
and things like that.
It was...
I just made up my mind when I went to Marine Corps.
I volunteered for the Marine Corps.
And they tried to break me, not me, all of us was in there doing it that particular time.
They tried to break us, but we all seemed to have the same thought in our mind.
We were there for a reason.
And nobody was going to break us regardless of what they did to us.
So that's what we did.
And I have a daughter just got out of Marine Corps.
She just put 20 years into Marine Corps.
And she often says, if it hadn't been for you and people like you, I wouldn't have been
here today.
And I've talked to other Marines, other young Marines, and I've got speeches on the base
of Black Marines and told them the hardship that we went through for them now, the opportunity
that they have now.
And they really appreciate it.
You know, you're just standing up.
How did the Negro League Players react in 1947 to the news of Jackie Robinson about the
color barrier?
Well, they act great.
They were happy.
But as I always say, you know, if you open up a crate out, there are always going to
be one or two bedrooms in there.
You know, not the same way with the Negro League Players.
There's always one or two.
And I should have made it.
I should have been picked.
But deep down in my heart, I think, Branch Rickie did the right thing, but picking Jackie
Robinson overall, other Negro League Players.
Because you must realize when Jackie went, broke the color barrier and it went to Montreal
to play.
All they wanted him to do is fight back or call names or something.
And they said, well, that's what you expect out of Blacks.
That's what you expect to be in an honest situation.
And he didn't do that.
And that's what broke the color barrier.
That's what went into the white society that he was there.
See, a white society said, the past time of baseball was for whites and whites only.
And then when Jackie Robinson went up there and broke the color barrier, they just opened
their mouth and couldn't believe it.
That's why they wrote, threatened the letters, death threats, and calling all kinds of
names.
And I can't believe that Baltimore City that treated him so bad.
That she was real life.
Baltimore, who went to the first southern states in the state of America and in the state history.
So it's a barrier, you know, and so I don't know.
It's just unbelievable that I said before.
It's unbelievable that you see one person, hate another person just because he's a color
of his skin.
You know, you take doing a war, take prince and doing a war.
They work side by side.
You know, Martin putting together planes and so on and so forth.
Any plant they work side by side.
And as soon everything was over, they went right back to the hating.
That's what I don't understand.
I watch TV and see where the hurricane went through.
Where a tornado.
And you see blacks down there and the whites hand to hand trying to help each other.
I know it's really going to get situated.
You're ready to call your nigger or a dorky and they won't want you in the store.
They want you in the house and things like that.
That's what I don't understand.
I never will understand it.
You mentioned in a pre-interview that you played in Canadian League.
Can you tell me how that came about?
Well I went in Canadian League in 1449 and I played for St. James.
St. James Braves.
And it was a good league up there.
Very good league.
And we had a white end black that played in it.
So B was a good league.
Everybody got along together.
I knew I had one manager that kind of got off the peak a little bit.
We overcame that.
You know.
He asked me to, I used the towels that the other white boys was using and he got me a cup
to drink out of it so I wouldn't drink out of the same cup.
But before the season was over I was drinking out of the same cup and using the same towels
because the type of men was up there.
They didn't let it continue.
Were the players in the Canadian League mostly Canadian?
No.
No.
We had a few Canadians.
What ways did your experiences as a Nigger League player change your life and your future?
Well, just like I said when I first started playing baseball in the Nigger League I signed
a contract and I was doing something that I lacked and I was getting paid for it.
It made me feel good to walk into Yankee Stadium with all the people there and be pitching
to the Yankee Stadium or any of the major league ballpark as far as that concerned.
It made me feel wonderful.
What type of attendance is for how many people would attend the games?
And you mentioned to me that you're doing a lot of things with the Nigger League now and
the schools can tell.
Yeah, well I go to schools and give talks and I go to car shows and sell the Nigger League
stuff.
I've got bats on my own and I still have pictures and baseball cards.
And just a little bit of everything of the Nigger League that I have myself.
Okay, Mr. Barb, thank you very much for your time.
Well, thank you.
Turn off the tape.
I'm resuming my interview with Mr. Ernest Bark.
Mr. Bark, could you tell me about spring training, how a pale black man would go about trying
out for a team and so forth?
Well, each team had its own town to spring training, a southern town because that's where
it was warm.
We used to spring train every March in Nashville, Tennessee.
Now, to order to make the team, it was just like the majors.
If a scout or somebody didn't see you, and offered you to come to Nashville, Tennessee
with the ball-releague giants, you just packed your bag and came down there and take your
chances.
And that's what it was all about.
You didn't just walk on a team just because one of the teams was playing just walked
back there and said that you said that you want to play, you would have to perform in
spring training and show that your ability was able enough to play in the Negro League.
And there's a lot of players that came that stayed in the minors.
Legs are what you call them in spring.
The scouting team was the second team to the ball-releague giants and they were playing
a southern leg until they were called up to the big team.
How about groupies?
Did you have groupies or did players have fan base and girls that would follow them trying
to come to the ball to the player?
Well, some guys did.
They didn't follow them because they wasn't that much money to follow them.
But sure enough, when you went to the same town again, you probably saw the same girl.
And you had fans, you had a lot of fans and especially a lot of kids.
I used to have a lot of kids.
I go to a town and the kids would all gang around me because I used to talk to them.
Although I didn't make much money, I used to take them out.
I had my screen and stuff like that and talk to them.
And that made me feel good.
It made me feel wonderful.
And to me, it made no difference whether it was white or black.
If they hadn't been in the group that I was there, they went just like everybody else.
Okay, Mr. Burke, you mentioned that when you were about 10 you moved to Canada.
Can you tell me what it was like living in Canada and how that compared with the United
States?
Well, it was quite a thrill because you never knew you were black, you looked in a mirror.
And everybody treated you regardless of what color you were.
Everybody treated you the same.
And I lived some people a fair called a Monarchs.
And they had three sons and two girls.
And they were just like brothers to me.
No matter how we used to call each other brothers in the system.
And they didn't get any more than I got.
I didn't get any more than they got.
We were all treated the same.
And it was really nice.
And the second world war broke out.
Then I came back to the States and volunteered and joined the Marine Corps.
And that was it from there.
Okay, on behalf of the University of Baltimore and the Bay Bridge Museum and myself, I'd
like to thank you for your time, Mr. Burke.
Thank you.
